AI.tldr: MLLMs struggle with part-level grounding due to missing object-part hierarchy. OP-HRG introduces coarse-to-fine reasoning-guided grounding with self-check mechanism and part-aware GRPO training. A 4B model trained this way outperforms 7B models and SAM3 on part segmentation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: MLLMs can ground whole objects well but fail at part-level queries because they lack object-part hierarchical reasoning.
AI.method: OP-HRG: two-stage coarse-to-fine grounding (parent object → part), self-check reflection with re-encoding of predicted crop, and part-aware GRPO framework with stage-wise rewards.
AI.result: A 4B model outperforms 7B grounding LLMs and SAM3 on PascalPart, PartImageNet, and InstructPart, and transfers to reasoning segmentation.
AI.conclusion: Explicit object-part hierarchy and reflective self-check significantly improve part-level grounding in MLLMs.

title: AnovaX: A Local, Multi-Agent Voice Assistant with LLM Planning, Typed Executors, and Adaptive Recovery
summary: Desktop voice assistants are still dominated by cloud pipelines that ship raw audio off the machine and expose a fixed set of skills. We describe AnovaX, a small local-first assistant that runs entirely on the user's computer and treats the desktop itself as its action surface. A single Python process wires together a wake-word gate, a speech pipeline, an LLM planner (Gemini) that emits a JSON plan of tool calls, a whitelist-and-denylist safety layer, a multi-agent orchestrator that translates each plan into typed child agents on a bounded thread pool, and an adaptive recovery loop that takes over whenever a core step fails. Every tool corresponds to a specialized agent class (AppAgent, TypingAgent, BrowserAgent and six others) with its own timeout, retry policy, and shared-resource locks. A recursive MetaAgent lets the planner delegate a sub-goal back to itself, capped at two levels of nesting. The recovery loop uses a compact ReAct-style prompt and hides Gemini's latency behind speculative execution of read-only tools. A companion Flask server exposes a phone-friendly remote over the local WiFi, mirrors every agent lifecycle event to the phone in real time, and streams the laptop's screen back over MJPEG so the user can watch remote commands land as they run. The point of the project is less to compete with Siri or Alexa than to show that a legible, few-thousand-line assistant is enough to open apps, type into them, run searches, coordinate concurrent actions, recover from single-step failures, and be driven entirely from a phone in another room -- without the LLM ever touching the keyboard.

AI.tldr: AnovaX is a local-first desktop voice assistant that runs entirely on the user's machine, using an LLM planner (Gemini) to generate JSON tool-call plans, a multi-agent orchestrator with specialized agents (AppAgent, TypingAgent, BrowserAgent, etc.), a safety layer, adaptive recovery, and a phone-friendly remote control via Flask/MJPEG — all in a few thousand lines of Python.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that a lightweight, local-first, legible assistant can replace cloud-dependent desktop voice assistants (Siri/Alexa) by treating the desktop itself as the action surface, without sending raw audio off the machine.
AI.method: A single Python process with: wake-word gate → speech pipeline → LLM planner (Gemini) emitting JSON tool plans → whitelist/denylist safety layer → multi-agent orchestrator (bounded thread pool) with typed agents (AppAgent, TypingAgent, BrowserAgent, etc.) → adaptive ReAct-style recovery loop; plus a Flask server for remote phone control via local WiFi with MJPEG screen streaming.
AI.result: The assistant can open apps, type into them, run searches, coordinate concurrent actions, recover from single-step failures, and be driven entirely from a phone in another room — without the LLM touching the keyboard directly.
AI.conclusion: A few-thousand-line, local-first architecture is sufficient to replicate core desktop assistant capabilities (multi-agent orchestration, safety, failure recovery, remote control) without cloud dependency, challenging the assumption that such systems require complex cloud pipelines.

title: Regularity-Aware Stochastic MGDA with Adaptive Conflict-Avoidant Update Direction Control
summary: Multi-objective learning (MOL) aims to optimize multiple objectives simultaneously. The multi-gradient descent algorithm (MGDA) is a workhorse that iteratively updates along a common descent or conflict-avoidant (CA) direction across objectives. In stochastic settings, however, the vanilla stochastic MGDA method, SMG, lacks a fast convergence rate because mini-batch sampling introduces noise in the gradients. This causes bias in the update direction, which is controlled by the CA direction continuity. In this paper, we show that the CA direction is $1/2$-Holder continuous with respect to the Jacobian matrix, and the exponent $1/2$ cannot be improved in the worst case. This leads to a suboptimal convergence rate for vanilla stochastic MGDA in prior works. Nevertheless, under additional regularity conditions, we show this can be improved to Lipschitz continuity. Based on this insight, we propose a stochastic multi-objective regularity-aware (MoRe) method that exploits the Lipschitz continuity of the CA direction when the subproblem is regular, and switches to a fixed scalarization weight otherwise. Intuitively, the proposed algorithm employs CA direction update when the gradient conflict is large, and linear scalarization update otherwise. Theoretically, our method improves the convergence rate of SMG in the nonconvex setting from $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(T^{-1/4})$ to $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(T^{-1/2})$, where $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(\cdot)$ hides logarithmic factors. Meanwhile, we also establish the per-iterate conflict-avoidance guarantees. Empirically, experiments demonstrate its effectiveness in multi-task performance and verify convergence behavior consistent with the established theoretical rate.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies that the conflict-avoidant (CA) direction in stochastic multi-gradient descent (SMG) has only $1/2$-Holder continuity, causing suboptimal convergence. It proposes MoRe, a method that switches between CA updates (when conflicts are large) and linear scalarization (when regular), achieving improved convergence from $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(T^{-1/4})$ to $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(T^{-1/2})$ in nonconvex settings, with per-iterate conflict-avoidance guarantees.
AI.motivation: Stochastic MGDA suffers from slow convergence due to noise-induced bias in the conflict-avoidant direction, and the underlying continuity properties of this direction were poorly understood, limiting theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: First proves CA direction is $1/2$-Holder continuous (tight) and can be Lipschitz under regularity. Then proposes MoRe: uses CA updates when gradient conflicts are large (non-regular), and switches to linear scalarization when the subproblem is regular, exploiting Lipschitz continuity for faster convergence.
AI.result: Convergence rate improved from $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(T^{-1/4})$ to $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(T^{-1/2})$ in nonconvex stochastic multi-objective learning. Per-iterate conflict-avoidance guarantees are established. Empirical results confirm effectiveness and match the theoretical rate.
AI.conclusion: Regularity-aware switching between conflict-avoidant and scalarization updates yields significantly faster convergence, providing both theoretical and empirical advances for stochastic multi-objective optimization.

title: A Variable-Length Gray Code for the Natural Numbers
summary: The reflected binary Gray code arranges the fixed-length binary representations of the integers so that consecutive numbers differ in a single bit. Its usefulness, however, is tied to a fixed word length $b$, which both caps the range of representable numbers at $2^{b}-1$ and wastes bits on small integers. We introduce a \emph{variable-length Gray code} $\V$, a total function from the natural numbers to the set of all finite binary strings, obtained by taking the reflected Gray code of $n+1$, discarding its leading zeros, and deleting the single leading one. We prove four properties of this construction. First, $\V$ is a bijection between the natural numbers and $\Bits$, so it is a complete code that assigns exactly one codeword to every finite binary string, including the empty string. Second, the Levenshtein (edit) distance between the codewords of two consecutive integers is always one; within a block of equal-length codewords the step is a single-bit substitution, and at each block boundary it is a single insertion. Third, codeword length is monotone non-decreasing in the encoded number and equals $\lfloor \log_2 (n+1) \rfloor$. Fourth, the transition from $(k-1)$-bit to $k$-bit codewords occurs exactly at $n=\sum_{i=0}^{k} 2^{i}=2^{k+1}-1$, where $\V(n)=1\,0^{k}$. The code therefore realises a Hamiltonian enumeration of $\Bits$ under unit edit steps while retaining a near-optimal, self-adapting length profile. We discuss its compression behaviour relative to fixed-length Gray codes and its application to the \emph{Isal*} family of instruction-set string encodings of graphs, hypergraphs, molecules and symbolic-regression expressions, in which the Levenshtein distance between encodings is used as a structural-similarity proxy.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a variable-length Gray code that maps natural numbers uniquely to all finite binary strings, with unit Levenshtein distance between consecutive codewords, monotonic length growth of ⌊log₂(n+1)⌋, and a near-optimal self-adapting length profile.
AI.motivation: Standard reflected binary Gray codes are limited by fixed word length b, capping representable numbers at 2^b-1 and wasting bits on small integers. A variable-length code is needed to efficiently represent all natural numbers without these constraints.
AI.method: The code V is constructed by taking the reflected Gray code of n+1, discarding leading zeros, and deleting the single leading one. This yields a bijection between natural numbers and all finite binary strings.
AI.result: Four properties are proved: (1) V is a bijection between ℕ and all finite binary strings; (2) Levenshtein distance between consecutive codewords is always 1 (substitution within equal-length blocks, insertion at boundaries); (3) codeword length = ⌊log₂(n+1)⌋, monotone non-decreasing; (4) transition to k-bit codewords occurs at n=2^{k+1}-1.
AI.conclusion: The code realizes a Hamiltonian enumeration of all finite binary strings under unit edit steps with a self-adapting length profile, and has applications in compression and the Isal* instruction-set string encodings where Levenshtein distance serves as a structural-similarity proxy.

title: Ask Twice, Look Twice: Prompt Echoing Resolves the Question-First Paradox in Vision-Language Models
summary: Where should the question go in a vision-language model (VLM) prompt: before the image or after it? Intuition says before: knowing what is asked should tell the model where to look. Yet across visual question answering benchmarks, question-first prompting consistently underperforms the image-first ordering recommended for frontier VLMs, a phenomenon we term the question-first paradox. We trace the paradox to a conflict between two stages of VLM computation. Logit-lens and attention probes show the intuition is half right: a question placed before the image genuinely steers perception, moving image patch representations toward question-relevant concepts. The failure lies downstream. Stranded behind hundreds of image tokens, the question is barely attended by the answer token, which instead commits to image-driven (often wrong) answers; a causal attention knockout confirms that the answer reads the question only when the question follows the image. The diagnosis yields a training-free fix: question echoing, restating the question on both sides of the image so that one copy steers perception while the other is read out at answer time. The same division of labor appears in a fifty-year-old finding on human ``adjunct questions'', where repeating a question before and after a passage aids comprehension more than either position alone. Echoing the image as well brings further gains, restoring the whole-image view a causal decoder otherwise loses. The paradox holds across five open VLMs, costing up to 17.5 group-accuracy points. Echoed prompts close it and surpass the best single-pass ordering on NaturalBench, POPE, Winoground, and open-ended VQAv2, by up to 19 Winoground group-accuracy points, with no training, fine-tuning, or architecture change. The paradox reveals a trade-off between steering perception and preserving question access; echoing resolves it through prompt design alone.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies the "question-first paradox" in VLMs—placing the question before the image (intuitive) actually underperforms image-first prompting. The cause is a trade-off: question-first helps steer perception but the answer token can't attend to the stranded question. The fix is "question echoing"—placing the question both before and after the image—which requires no training and closes performance gaps of up to 17.5 accuracy points.
AI.motivation: To resolve a counterintuitive phenomenon in VLM prompting: despite intuition that asking a question before showing an image should guide visual attention, question-first prompting consistently underperforms image-first ordering in practice. The authors seek to understand and fix this paradox.
AI.method: Using logit-lens and attention probes to analyze the two-stage computation conflict, causal attention knockout experiments, and proposing a training-free "question echoing" prompt design (question before and after image). Also echoes the image for additional gains.
AI.result: Question echoing closes the paradox and surpasses best single-pass ordering on NaturalBench, POPE, Winoground, and open-ended VQAv2, gaining up to 19 Winoground group-accuracy points across five open VLMs, with no training or architecture changes.
AI.conclusion: The question-first paradox reveals an inherent trade-off between steering perception and preserving question access for the answer token. Echoed prompts resolve this through prompt design alone, drawing a parallel to 50-year-old findings on human "adjunct questions" where repeating questions aids comprehension.

title: Optimal Sampling and Reconstruction of Graph Signals in the Fractional Fourier Domain
summary: Graph signal sampling and reconstruction are commonly formulated in the graph Fourier transform (GFT) domain. However, the reconstruction performance may be limited when practical graph signals are not sufficiently concentrated in the GFT spectrum. To address this issue, this paper proposes a graph signal sampling and reconstruction framework based on the graph fractional Fourier transform (GFRFT) domain. The fractional order is introduced as an adjustable spectral domain parameter, and the optimal order is selected to provide a more suitable representation domain for a given graph signal and sampling model. Under a unified sampling reconstruction formulation, subspace, smoothness, and stochastic priors are incorporated, and both unconstrained and predefined reconstruction mechanisms are considered, leading to several fractional domain sampling and reconstruction methods. Furthermore, the theoretical analysis shows that the optimal GFRFT domain can provide a more suitable low-dimensional spectral representation by improving energy concentration and reducing projection residual. The effects of residual leakage and noise amplification are further considered to explain how this representation advantage is translated into reconstruction error reduction. Experimental results show that, GFRFT domain sampling and reconstruction generally achieve better recovery performance than GFT domain methods.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a graph signal sampling and reconstruction framework using the Graph Fractional Fourier Transform (GFRFT) domain, which introduces an adjustable fractional order to achieve better spectral concentration and reconstruction performance compared to traditional GFT-based methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional GFT-domain sampling and reconstruction perform poorly when graph signals are not sufficiently concentrated in the GFT spectrum, limiting reconstruction accuracy.
AI.method: A GFRFT-based sampling and reconstruction framework is developed with an adjustable fractional order parameter. It incorporates subspace, smoothness, and stochastic priors under both unconstrained and predefined reconstruction mechanisms, with optimal order selection to improve spectral concentration.
AI.result: The optimal GFRFT domain provides better energy concentration and lower projection residual, reducing residual leakage and noise amplification. Experimental results show superior recovery performance over GFT-domain methods.
AI.conclusion: The GFRFT domain offers a more flexible and effective representation for graph signal sampling and reconstruction, outperforming conventional GFT-based approaches.

title: Risk-Aware Preference Learning for Stochastic Outcomes
comment: ICRA 2026 Workshop on Bridging the Gap between Robot Learning and Human-Robot Interaction
summary: Learning reward functions from human preferences is a widely used approach for aligning robot behavior with user expectations in human-robot interaction. Most existing approaches assume that humans evaluate uncertain outcomes using expected utility (EU), aggregating outcome utilities linearly with their probabilities. However, behavioral evidence shows that humans are systematically risk-sensitive, overweighting rare negative events and exhibiting loss aversion. We study the consequences of this mismatch in social robot navigation, where safety-critical outcomes (e.g., collisions) are rare but highly consequential. We compare EU with Cumulative Prospect Theory (CPT), a nonlinear model of human decision-making, within a Bradley-Terry preference learning framework. Our preliminary experiments show that when preferences are generated by risk-sensitive users, CPT-based learners recover reward functions with substantially lower regret compared to EU-based learners. Our results highlight the importance of modeling human risk sensitivity when learning rewards from preferences over stochastic robot outcomes.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that modeling human risk sensitivity using Cumulative Prospect Theory (CPT) rather than Expected Utility (EU) significantly improves reward function learning from human preferences in social robot navigation, especially when users exhibit risk-sensitive behavior toward rare but consequential outcomes like collisions.
AI.motivation: Standard reward learning from preferences assumes humans evaluate outcomes using Expected Utility (EU), which linearly aggregates utilities with probabilities. However, behavioral evidence shows humans are risk-sensitive—overweighting rare negative events and exhibiting loss aversion. This mismatch is problematic for safety-critical human-robot interaction scenarios like social navigation where collisions are rare but severe.
AI.method: The authors compare Expected Utility (EU) against Cumulative Prospect Theory (CPT)—a nonlinear model of human decision-making—within a Bradley-Terry preference learning framework. They conduct preliminary experiments where preferences are generated by risk-sensitive users in stochastic robot navigation scenarios.
AI.result: CPT-based learners recover reward functions with substantially lower regret compared to EU-based learners when preferences come from risk-sensitive users.
AI.conclusion: Modeling human risk sensitivity is crucial for learning accurate reward functions from preferences over stochastic robot outcomes, particularly in safety-critical domains.

title: Partial Information Decomposition as a Multi-Contrast 3D MRI Selection Strategy for Resource-Constrained Deep Neural Network Training in Brain Tumor Segmentation
summary: Multi-contrast 3D MRI segmentation can be computationally demanding when all available sequences are used. We evaluate a pre-training Partial Information Decomposition framework that ranks input pairs according to their redundant, unique, and synergistic information about regional tumor burden and selects the highest-ranked pair for downstream training. Applied to T1n, T1c, T2w, and T2-FLAIR MRI, the framework selected T1c+T2-FLAIR. We then trained eleven architecturally identical lightweight 3D U-Nets using different input configurations. On an independent test cohort, T1c+T2-FLAIR was the strongest two-input configuration and ranked second overall in mean Dice (0.676 versus 0.687 for all four inputs). Independent Shapley analysis on the full-input model also identified T2-FLAIR and T1c as the most influential inputs and their pairwise interaction as the strongest. These findings demonstrate the practical value of PID based pre-training selection for identifying compact, informative MRI input sets before costly 3D model development.

AI.tldr: A Partial Information Decomposition (PID) framework pre-selects optimal MRI input pairs for 3D segmentation, identifying T1c+T2-FLAIR as the best two-input combination—nearly matching full four-input performance (Dice 0.676 vs 0.687)—reducing computational cost before model training.
AI.motivation: Multi-contrast 3D MRI segmentation using all available sequences is computationally demanding; the authors aim to reduce input dimensionality while maintaining segmentation accuracy.
AI.method: Use a PID framework to rank MRI input pairs by redundant, unique, and synergistic information about tumor burden; select top pair (T1c+T2-FLAIR); train eleven lightweight 3D U-Nets with different input configurations; validate with Shapley analysis.
AI.result: T1c+T2-FLAIR achieved mean Dice 0.676 (best two-input), nearly matching the four-input model's 0.687. Shapley analysis confirmed T2-FLAIR and T1c as most influential inputs with strongest pairwise interaction.
AI.conclusion: PID-based pre-selection effectively identifies compact, informative MRI input sets, reducing computational burden before costly 3D model development without significant performance loss.

title: Precise but Uncoupled: Reviewer Precision Does Not Guarantee Critique Uptake in Multi-Agent Math Reasoning
comment: 48 pages, 20 figures, 25 tables. Public release website: https://huggingface.co/spaces/AgentsSci/scientific-agent-protocol-traces-site
summary: Many math- and science-oriented agent systems use hierarchical designs with specialized reviewer roles, assuming that a dedicated review stage should help turn wrong candidates into correct ones. We test this assumption on 4,181 verifier-grounded Omni-MATH problems using matched gpt-oss-120b actors. Collaboration adds little on the easiest tiers, but from tier 4 onward the gains open sharply; in this harder regime, broadcast-style peer discussion reaches higher final accuracy than a planner-executor-reviewer pipeline (PER). We ask whether this gap is explained by reviewer quality or by whether critique changes the next answer the protocol carries forward. It is not explained by reviewer precision alone: PER's reviewer is more precise than broadcast's (0.861 vs. 0.644), yet evaluator-verified useful critique is much less likely to change the next candidate and produces lower reviewer-guided repair. These results show that reviewer detection quality and critique uptake are empirically separable. Within matched PER interventions, forcing explicit acknowledgment lowers final accuracy, while embedding reviewer guidance directly in the solver's working context partially improves follow-through without closing the gap. Overall, reviewer-centric evaluation can overstate system quality: a protocol may spot errors well yet still fail to solve more problems if it does not act on those critiques.

AI.tldr: A study of 4,181 math problems finds that broadcast-style peer discussion outperforms hierarchical planner-executor-reviewer pipelines (PER) because, despite PER's reviewer being more precise, its critiques are less likely to change subsequent answers and produce less effective repair.
AI.motivation: To test the common assumption in math/science agent systems that dedicated reviewer stages help turn wrong candidates into correct ones, and to understand why some protocols outperform others.
AI.method: Tested 4,181 Omni-MATH problems with matched gpt-oss-120b actors, comparing broadcast-style peer discussion vs. planner-executor-reviewer pipeline (PER), with ablation studies on reviewer quality, critique uptake, acknowledgment forcing, and embedding guidance.
AI.result: Broadcast outperforms PER from tier 4 onward despite PER having more precise reviewers (0.861 vs 0.644). PER's critiques are less likely to change subsequent candidates and produce lower repair. Forcing acknowledgment lowers accuracy; embedding guidance partially helps but doesn't close the gap.
AI.conclusion: Reviewer detection quality and critique uptake are empirically separable; reviewer-centric evaluation can overstate system quality since a protocol that spots errors well may still fail to solve problems if it doesn't act on critiques effectively.

title: AI Trading: Evaluating Large Language Models for Technical Market Analysis
comment: Master's research paper, Georgia Institute of Technology. 31 pages, 4 figures
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for processing the heterogeneous information environments of modern financial markets. This paper presents a systematic, comparative evaluation of five prominent LLMs: GPT-4 Turbo, Claude 3 Opus, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Llama 3 70B, and the domain-specialized FinGPT, with respect to their capacity for technical market analysis. The evaluation spans four structured tasks: candlestick pattern recognition from OHLCV data, directional signal generation (BUY/SELL/HOLD), backtesting of signal quality through a simulated execution pipeline, and financial report comprehension. Our experimental framework employs rigorous quantitative metrics, including Sharpe ratio, maximum drawdown, Sortino ratio, information coefficient, F1-score, and BLEU score. Findings from simulated backtesting indicate that GPT-4 Turbo achieves the highest annualized return and Sharpe ratio among general-purpose models, while FinGPT demonstrates competitive risk-adjusted performance due to domain-specific fine-tuning. Both models outperform a passive S&P 500 benchmark under the tested conditions. The study identifies persistent failure modes across all evaluated models, including numerical hallucination, context-window limitations, and inconsistent performance in sideways market regimes. We conclude that while LLMs hold genuine promise within AI trading systems, robust deployment requires careful task decomposition, rigorous backtesting protocols, and domain-aware fine-tuning strategies.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates five LLMs (GPT-4 Turbo, Claude 3 Opus, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Llama 3 70B, FinGPT) on technical market analysis tasks, finding GPT-4 Turbo best among general models and FinGPT strongest for risk-adjusted returns, though all models suffer from numerical hallucination and inconsistent performance.
AI.motivation: To systematically assess the capacity of modern LLMs for technical market analysis in financial markets, given their emerging role in processing heterogeneous financial information.
AI.method: A structured comparative evaluation across four tasks (candlestick pattern recognition, directional signal generation, backtesting via simulated execution, financial report comprehension), using quantitative metrics (Sharpe ratio, max drawdown, Sortino ratio, IC, F1, BLEU) against a passive S&P 500 benchmark.
AI.result: GPT-4 Turbo achieved the highest annualized return and Sharpe ratio among general-purpose models; FinGPT showed competitive risk-adjusted performance via domain fine-tuning; all models exhibited numerical hallucination, context-window limits, and poor sideways-market performance.
AI.conclusion: LLMs show genuine promise for AI trading systems, but practical deployment requires task decomposition, rigorous backtesting, and domain-aware fine-tuning to address identified failure modes.

title: DebrisTracer: Reliable Tracking in Hypervelocity Impact Fast Imaging
comment: 14 pages (12 + appendix), 16 figures. Accepted at IEEE VIS 2026. To appear in IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics (TVCG)
summary: This application paper presents DebrisTracer, a framework for the reliable tracking of debris in hypervelocity impact fast imaging. These noisy and highly specific datasets capture the ejection of a large number of debris fragments after the impact of a projectile launched at hypervelocity into a target material. The reliable estimation of debris mass and speed distributions is of major importance in aerospace applications. We document how to extend an off-the-shelf topology tracking framework based on critical point extraction and matching, in order to incorporate domain knowledge and physical assumptions. Our approach automatically produces an accurate and reliable debris tracking, enabling an interpretable visual analysis of this complex space-time phenomenon. Extensive experiments demonstrate the accuracy improvements provided by our approach over established tools used by domain experts in terms of physical validation, specifically via the prediction of the experimental ejected mass and crater depth profiles. We illustrate the utility of our approach across several use cases (with varying impact angles and physics). We show that our statistical summaries enable the visual identification of distinct regimes within the debris population, corroborating and refining prior expectations of domain experts. Our database and our C++ implementation are available at this address: https://github.com/tloloum/DebrisTracer.

AI.tldr: DebrisTracer is a framework that extends topology tracking with domain knowledge to automatically, accurately, and reliably track debris fragments in hypervelocity impact imaging, enabling interpretable visual analysis and improved physical validation of ejected mass and crater depth predictions.
AI.motivation: Reliable estimation of debris mass and speed distributions is critical for aerospace applications, but existing tools lack accuracy and interpretability on noisy, highly specific hypervelocity impact datasets.
AI.method: Extends an off-the-shelf topology tracking framework based on critical point extraction and matching, incorporating domain knowledge and physical assumptions to produce accurate debris tracking.
AI.result: Achieves significant accuracy improvements over established expert tools in physical validation (ejected mass and crater depth profiles), enables visual identification of distinct debris regimes, and works across varying impact angles and physics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach automatically produces accurate and reliable debris tracking with interpretable visual analysis, corroborating and refining prior expert expectations; implementation and database are open-sourced.

title: FSIM: Fluid Element Stacked Intelligent Metasurface for Multiuser Downlink Networks
summary: A fluid element (FE)-aided stacked intelligent metasurface (FSIM) for multiple-input single-output (MISO) communication system is investigated, where a multi-antenna base station (BS) serves multiple single-antenna users through FSIM. Unlike conventional SIM with fixed meta-atom deployment, the architecture allows the meta-atoms in each layer to move within a predefined fluidic region to further increase the spatial diversity. By jointly optimizing the two-dimensional meta-atom positions, BS transmit beamforming, and FSIM phase-shifts, the cascaded BS-FSIM-user channels can be flexibly reconfigured to enhance the desired signals and suppress multiuser interference. The proposed sum-rate maximization problem is highly non-convex and nonlinear due to the coupled solutions of element position, beamforming, and phase-shift. To address this challenge, an alternating optimization (AO) algorithm is developed to iteratively update these variables. The beamforming and FSIM phase-shift subproblems are transformed into semi-definite programming problems and solved by using successive convex approximation (SCA), first-order Taylor approximation, and penalty-based rank-one relaxation, whilst the FE position subproblem is handled through a projected gradient-based update. Simulation results reveal that a compact FSIM with a small inter-layer thickness is preferable, as increasing the thickness weakens inter-layer coupling and degrades the achievable sum rate. Results also demonstrate that the proposed FSIM significantly outperforms conventional SIMs with fixed positions, patch-based structures, partial fluidity, restricted fluid regions, and existing flexible intelligent metasurfaces. Furthermore, the proposed AO-based algorithm achieves superior rate performance compared to sub-schemes, metaheuristic methods, and conventional beamforming benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a fluid element-aided stacked intelligent metasurface (FSIM) for MISO systems, where meta-atoms can move within predefined fluidic regions to enhance spatial diversity. An alternating optimization algorithm jointly optimizes meta-atom positions, beamforming, and phase-shifts to maximize sum-rate, significantly outperforming conventional SIMs.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of conventional SIMs with fixed meta-atom deployments, and to further increase spatial diversity and flexibility in reconfiguring wireless channels for multiuser MISO systems.
AI.method: Proposes FSIM architecture with movable meta-atoms; formulates a sum-rate maximization problem with coupled variables; develops an AO algorithm that transforms beamforming and phase-shift subproblems into SDP problems solved via SCA, Taylor approximation, and penalty-based rank-one relaxation; handles FE position subproblem via projected gradient-based update.
AI.result: Compact FSIM with small inter-layer thickness is preferable; FSIM significantly outperforms conventional SIMs with fixed positions, patch-based structures, partial fluidity, restricted regions, and existing flexible metasurfaces; the AO algorithm achieves superior rate over sub-schemes, metaheuristics, and conventional beamforming.
AI.conclusion: FSIM with movable meta-atoms offers a promising architecture for future wireless systems, enabling flexible channel reconfiguration and substantial sum-rate gains over rigid metasurface designs.

title: Environment Design for Reliable Shared Autonomy with Probabilistic Guarantees
comment: ICRA 2026 Workshop Shared Challenges in Human-Centered and Resilient Robotic Autonomy
summary: Shared autonomy enables humans and robots to collaboratively perform tasks by combining human input with autonomous assistance. Most prior work focuses on improving intent inference under a fixed environment, overlooking how workspace design itself affects inference difficulty. We observe that the physical arrangement of objects directly influences the separability of candidate goals under noisy user inputs. We formulate workspace design as an optimization problem and derive a probabilistic correctness guarantee under a bounded noise model. Through simulation experiments across multiple tabletop scenarios, we show that optimized layouts improve goal inference reliability and reduce ambiguity compared to baseline arrangements. We further demonstrate a real-world shared autonomy system that integrates the proposed inference framework. This highlights the role of environment design as a complementary axis for improving shared autonomy systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes optimizing workspace layouts to improve goal inference reliability in shared autonomy systems, demonstrating through simulations and real-world experiments that strategic object arrangement reduces ambiguity under noisy user inputs.
AI.motivation: Prior shared autonomy work focuses on improving intent inference under fixed environments, but ignores how workspace design itself affects inference difficulty with noisy inputs.
AI.method: Formulates workspace design as an optimization problem with a probabilistic correctness guarantee under bounded noise; evaluated through simulations and a real-world shared autonomy system.
AI.result: Optimized layouts improve goal inference reliability and reduce ambiguity compared to baseline arrangements in tabletop scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Environment design serves as a complementary axis for improving shared autonomy systems beyond algorithmic inference improvements.

title: Unsupervised Keypoints for Real-Time Fall Detection: Comparative Analysis Under Real-world Conditions with Predictive Bandwidth Reduction
summary: Falls among older adults are a major safety challenge, but continuous monitoring is difficult to sustain. Video captures fall-related posture and motion, yet deployment is limited by privacy, computation, and bandwidth. Supervised pose estimation is anatomically interpretable but vulnerable to occlusion and partial body visibility. We propose a privacy-preserving framework that replaces RGB transmission with compact motion representations based on unsupervised keypoints and predictive temporal modeling. Local processing performs segmentation and keypoint extraction; variational recurrent prediction and sequence classification then detect falls from observed and forecasted motion. We evaluate the framework on the UR Fall Detection and Human Fall datasets using random, subject-disjoint, and occlusion-based splits. Under random splits, neither representation consistently dominates, suggesting that standard protocols may hide meaningful differences. Under subject-disjoint evaluation, supervised keypoints show a statistically significant advantage, but performance varies by subject: they perform better when anatomical landmarks are visible, whereas unsupervised keypoints are more robust to occlusion and partial visibility, though they produce more false positives for complex activities. Under occlusion-based evaluation, supervised keypoints miss nearly half of all falls, while unsupervised keypoints retain strong sensitivity and substantially outperform them. Their anatomical independence allows spatial anchors to adapt to visible body structure rather than fail on absent landmarks. The gap widens under bandwidth constraints, where supervised localization errors compound through the temporal model. These findings show that representation choice should reflect expected visual conditions and that unsupervised keypoints offer an advantage when body visibility is compromised.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a privacy-preserving fall detection framework using unsupervised keypoints and predictive temporal modeling, showing that unsupervised keypoints outperform supervised ones under occlusion and bandwidth constraints while supervised keypoints work better when anatomical landmarks are visible.
AI.motivation: Falls among older adults are a major safety concern, but continuous video monitoring is limited by privacy, computation, and bandwidth issues. Supervised pose estimation is vulnerable to occlusion and partial body visibility, motivating a privacy-preserving alternative.
AI.method: The framework replaces RGB transmission with compact motion representations based on unsupervised keypoints and predictive temporal modeling. Local processing performs segmentation and keypoint extraction; variational recurrent prediction and sequence classification detect falls from observed and forecasted motion.
AI.result: Under subject-disjoint evaluation, supervised keypoints show a significant advantage when anatomical landmarks are visible. Under occlusion-based evaluation, supervised keypoints miss nearly half of all falls, while unsupervised keypoints retain strong sensitivity. The gap widens under bandwidth constraints.
AI.conclusion: Representation choice should reflect expected visual conditions; unsupervised keypoints offer a clear advantage when body visibility is compromised, such as in occlusion or bandwidth-limited scenarios.

title: DrawingVQA: A Real-World Benchmark for Multi-Depth Visual-Textual Reasoning on Construction Drawings
summary: We introduce DrawingVQA, the first benchmark designed to evaluate multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on real-world construction drawings -- a core media in architecture, civil, and many other engineering practices. Unlike natural images or schematic floor plans, construction drawings fuse abstract geometry, symbolic notation, tabular data, annotations, and domain-specific text, forming a uniquely complex visual-textual domain core to engineering workflows. DrawingVQA bridges this gap with 33 "Issued for Construction" drawings and 92 expertly curated question-answer pairs, spanning three reasoning depths: perceptual understanding, contextual interpretation, and domain-expert reasoning. To evaluate model capabilities, we present a dual categorization framework to jointly analyze performance across seven construction-engineering and four MLLM capability dimensions -- the first to explicitly map engineering workflows to AI reasoning competencies. Evaluations of state-of-the-art MLLMs reveal a substantial gap between model and expert performance, particularly at higher reasoning depths. This benchmark lays a foundation for domain-specialized multimodal reasoning to allow for advancement on integration of AI-driven understanding and real-world engineering workflows.

AI.tldr: DrawingVQA is a new benchmark using real construction drawings (33 drawings, 92 QA pairs) to evaluate MLLMs across three reasoning depths, revealing a significant gap between model and expert performance, especially in higher-level reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLM benchmarks focus on natural images or simple floor plans, lacking evaluation on real construction drawings — which combine abstract geometry, symbols, tables, annotations, and domain text — despite their centrality to engineering workflows.
AI.method: The authors collected 33 "Issued for Construction" drawings and 92 expert-curated QA pairs, categorized by three reasoning depths (perceptual, contextual, domain-expert) and analyzed via a dual categorization framework mapping seven engineering dimensions to four MLLM capability dimensions.
AI.result: State-of-the-art MLLMs showed a substantial performance gap compared to human experts, especially at higher reasoning depths (contextual interpretation and domain-expert reasoning).
AI.conclusion: DrawingVQA establishes a foundation for domain-specialized multimodal reasoning, highlighting the need for AI to better integrate with real-world engineering workflows.

title: qZACH-ViT: Quantization-Aware Intrinsic Explanations with Recursive Attribution-Stabilized Optimization
comment: 31 pages, 8 figures, 10 tables. Includes executable mixed-precision ONNX INT8 deployment validation across 210 checkpoints
summary: Compact medical-image classifiers need efficiency and interpretable evidence, yet these goals are often addressed separately. We introduce qZACH-ViT, a quantization-aware extension of the zero-token (CLS-token-free), position-free ZACH-ViT backbone with recursive intrinsic patch-level class evidence. We also introduce Recursive Attribution-Stabilized Optimization (RASO), which norm-matches classification and attribution gradients and removes attribution components that conflict with classification. We evaluate four controlled conditions on seven MedMNIST datasets using 50 training images per class and ten fixed seeds, completing 280 runs. All 210 qZACH-ViT checkpoints are converted to executable mixed-precision ONNX INT8 graphs containing 16 signed INT8 MatMulInteger projections with INT32 accumulation. Deployed mixed-precision INT8 qZACH-ViT with Adam improves the FP32 ZACH-ViT baseline mean on all seven datasets, with a mean paired gain of 0.0313 in the dataset-specific primary metric; qZACH-ViT with RASO yields a mean gain of 0.0368. Across 964,920 source-to-INT8 test comparisons, prediction agreement is 99.9751\%, with a mean absolute primary-metric change of 0.000133 and a maximum of 0.004386. Across 3,600 matched intrinsic maps, mean cosine similarity is 0.999955, mean rank correlation is 0.9944, and mean top-10\% overlap is 0.9692. ONNX artifacts are 70.0\% smaller than source checkpoints and provide $1.41\times$ and $2.39\times$ end-to-end CPU speedups with one and four threads. RASO significantly reduces sufficiency error and improves input-noise stability over Adam with the same attribution loss, but does not dominate every predictive or explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) metric. These results establish qZACH-ViT as a deployable compact intrinsically explainable model and RASO as a targeted stability-oriented optimization procedure.

AI.tldr: qZACH-ViT is a quantization-aware, compact, intrinsically explainable ViT backbone that, combined with RASO optimization, achieves mixed-precision INT8 deployment with near-perfect prediction agreement, high attribution stability, smaller model size, CPU speedups, and improved performance over the FP32 baseline across MedMNIST datasets.
AI.motivation: Compact medical-image classifiers need both efficiency and interpretable evidence, but these goals are typically addressed separately. The paper aims to unify them in a single framework.
AI.method: Introduces qZACH-ViT (quantization-aware, zero-token, position-free ViT) and Recursive Attribution-Stabilized Optimization (RASO), which norm-matches classification and attribution gradients and removes conflicting attribution components. Models are converted to mixed-precision INT8 ONNX graphs.
AI.result: On seven MedMNIST datasets (50 training images/class, 280 runs), qZACH-ViT with Adam gains +0.0313 mean improvement over FP32 baseline; with RASO gains +0.0368. Prediction agreement 99.9751%, cosine similarity of intrinsic maps 0.999955, ONNX artifacts 70% smaller, 1.41×/2.39× CPU speedups (1/4 threads). RASO reduces sufficiency error and improves noise stability.
AI.conclusion: qZACH-ViT is a deployable, compact, intrinsically explainable model; RASO is a targeted stability-oriented optimization procedure. The approach successfully unifies efficiency and interpretability for medical image classification.

title: Physics-Based Deep Spatiotemporal Hyperlocal Radar Nowcasting with a Multi-Variable U-Net for High-Resolution Precipitation Forecasting
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
summary: Precipitation nowcasting over the immediate 10-90 min period is important for flood management and real-time decision-making in urban regions. Conventional short-range forecasting with high-resolution numerical weather prediction requires frequent data assimilation, model initialization, and spin-up, introducing computational latency. Machine learning provides an alternative by learning storm evolution directly from high-frequency observations and producing forecasts quickly after training. This is particularly relevant for Mumbai, India, where monsoon convection, land-sea interactions, and localized intense rainfall make short-term prediction difficult. Here, we develop a compact radar-only nowcasting framework that combines multi-elevation reflectivity, Doppler radial velocity, and radial-velocity-gradient proxy features within an encoder-decoder U-Net. Using the most recent radar volume scan, the model predicts 12 future composite reflectivity fields at 7.5-min intervals up to 90 min lead time. The derived velocity magnitude, divergence-like, directional-shear, and vorticity-like channels represent kinematic signatures associated with convergence and boundary interactions without requiring full wind-field retrieval. A high-reflectivity attention module improves sensitivity to convective cores, and physics-guided attribution examines whether the learned sensitivities are meteorologically meaningful. The model is trained using Mumbai Doppler radar observations from May to August 2023 and evaluated on temporally independent events. At 90 min lead time, Critical Success Index values are 0.437, 0.332, and 0.193 for $\geq$10, $\geq$20, and $\geq$30 dBZ thresholds, respectively. Compared with persistence, the model gives lower RMSE and higher spatial correlation at longer lead times. Once trained, it runs on a standard computer, generating nowcasts within seconds for real-time use.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a compact radar-only U-Net nowcasting framework for Mumbai that predicts 90-minute precipitation from multi-elevation radar data using derived kinematic proxy features and a high-reflectivity attention module, achieving real-time performance with competitive skill scores.
AI.motivation: Short-term precipitation nowcasting (10-90 min) is critical for urban flood management, especially in complex monsoon regions like Mumbai. Conventional NWP methods suffer from computational latency, while existing ML approaches often lack physics-informed design tailored to local convective dynamics.
AI.method: An encoder-decoder U-Net framework using multi-elevation radar reflectivity, Doppler velocity, and proxy kinematic features (velocity magnitude, divergence-like, directional-shear, vorticity-like channels) without full wind retrieval. A high-reflectivity attention module enhances convective core sensitivity. Physics-guided attribution validates meteorological meaningfulness.
AI.result: At 90 min lead time, CSI values of 0.437, 0.332, and 0.193 for ≥10, ≥20, ≥30 dBZ thresholds. Lower RMSE and higher spatial correlation than persistence at longer lead times. Runs in seconds on a standard computer after training.
AI.conclusion: The compact radar-only approach provides a computationally efficient, real-time capable alternative for urban precipitation nowcasting in complex monsoon environments, with meteorologically interpretable learned features.

title: Energy-Efficient Resource Allocation for Six-Dimensional Movable Antenna Systems
summary: This paper investigates the energy-efficiency (EE) maximization problem for a multiuser wireless network equipped with six-dimensional movable antennas (6DMAs), where the three-dimensional (3D) positions and orientations of the antennas are jointly optimized to fully exploit the additional spatial degrees of freedom offered by dynamic channel reconfiguration. However, the practical operation of 6DMAs incurs non-negligible mechanical energy consumption. Moreover, orientation-dependent phase variations, together with the strong coupling among antenna positions, rotation angles, transmit beamforming, and time allocation, render the resulting problem highly non-convex and analytically challenging. To address this issue, we develop a block coordinate descent (BCD) optimization framework that integrates Dinkelbach's transformation with the majorization-minimization (MM) approach to efficiently obtain a high quality suboptimal solution with guaranteed convergence. Simulation results unveil that the proposed design achieves significant EE improvements over conventional benchmarks, thereby highlighting the critical importance of accounting for practical mechanical energy costs in 6DMA enabled systems. Furthermore, our results reveal a fundamental trade-off between throughput enhancement and mechanical overhead: although larger antenna reconfigurations can improve channel conditions, their EE gains gradually diminish due to the increased mechanical energy consumption.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a BCD-based optimization framework integrating Dinkelbach's transformation and MM approach to maximize energy-efficiency in 6DMA systems, revealing a trade-off between throughput gains and mechanical energy costs.
AI.motivation: To maximize energy-efficiency in 6DMA-enabled multiuser wireless networks by jointly optimizing 3D positions, orientations, beamforming, and time allocation, while accounting for non-negligible mechanical energy consumption.
AI.method: A block coordinate descent (BCD) optimization framework integrating Dinkelbach's transformation with the majorization-minimization (MM) approach to handle the highly non-convex problem.
AI.result: The proposed design achieves significant EE improvements over conventional benchmarks, revealing a fundamental trade-off where larger antenna reconfigurations improve channel conditions but EE gains diminish due to increased mechanical energy consumption.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for practical mechanical energy costs is critical in 6DMA-enabled systems, as the EE gains from antenna reconfiguration diminish with excessive mechanical overhead.

title: Multi-Objective Kinodynamic Motion Planning with Asymptotic Pareto Optimality
summary: In this paper, we address the challenge of multi-objective motion planning for systems under kinodynamic constraints. We consider three problem classes: (i) lexicographic optimization, in which objectives are minimized according to a strict priority ordering, (ii) constrained optimization, in which a primary objective is minimized subject to bounds on the remaining costs, and (iii) Pareto front optimization, in which the goal is to approximate the full set of optimal trade-offs among competing objectives. We first show that established cost scalarization methods for multi-objective problems cannot be extended to continuous-domain systems with correctness guarantees. Then, we propose a unified algorithmic framework built upon the Stable Sparse-RRT (SST) algorithm, in which the single representative maintained at each witness neighborhood is replaced by a representative set of locally Pareto-optimal nodes. This structure gives rise to three distinct algorithms: lexSST for lexicographic minimization, coSST for constrained optimization, and poSST for Pareto-front approximation. We provide theoretical guarantees for the completeness and optimality of our algorithms and demonstrate their effectiveness through extensive empirical evaluations.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a unified multi-objective motion planning framework (lexSST, coSST, poSST) built on SST, addressing lexicographic, constrained, and Pareto front optimization under kinodynamic constraints, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Existing cost scalarization methods for multi-objective problems cannot be extended to continuous-domain systems with correctness guarantees, motivating the need for a new principled approach.
AI.method: The authors extend the Stable Sparse-RRT (SST) algorithm by replacing the single representative at each witness node with a set of locally Pareto-optimal nodes, yielding three variants: lexSST (lexicographic), coSST (constrained), and poSST (Pareto front approximation).
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees of completeness and optimality are provided, and extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate effectiveness across the three problem classes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unified framework successfully handles multi-objective kinodynamic motion planning with formal guarantees, overcoming the limitations of scalarization-based approaches.

title: GS-RealBlur: A Flexible Data Acquisition Framework for Real-World Image Deblurring
summary: High-quality, large-scale paired data is essential for training learning-based image deblurring models. However, synthetic blurry images generally lack realism, while real-world captured images require complex and inflexible camera systems. In this work, we propose GS-RealBlur, a data acquisition framework for real-world image deblurring, achieving both blur realism and acquisition flexibility. Specifically, we use a handheld camera to capture blurry images, and deploy a gimbal to densely capture sharp images of the same scene. We reconstruct the 3D representation of sharp images and calibrate the camera pose of each blurry frame within this 3D. The image rendered from this 3D according to the pose serves as the sharp counterpart. To better align the rendered image with the blurry image, we introduce a Blur-aware Pose Refinement (BPR) module that refines the pose using appearance consistency and centroid alignment constraints. Leveraging GS-RealBlur, we construct a high-quality and diverse dataset. Extensive experiments demonstrate that a deblurring model trained on our dataset achieves superior generalization performance across various real-world deblurring benchmarks, consistently outperforming models trained on existing synthetic and real-world datasets. The code and dataset will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: GS-RealBlur is a data acquisition framework that uses handheld cameras for blurry images and a gimbal for sharp images, reconstructs 3D sharp scenes, and refines poses via Blur-aware Pose Refinement to create realistic paired datasets for training deblurring models, achieving superior generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing paired datasets for image deblurring suffer from either lack of realism (synthetic) or inflexible acquisition (complex camera systems). The authors aim to achieve both blur realism and acquisition flexibility.
AI.method: (1) Handheld camera captures blurry images; gimbal captures dense sharp images. (2) 3D representation of sharp images is reconstructed. (3) Camera pose of each blurry frame is calibrated within this 3D. (4) Blur-aware Pose Refinement (BPR) module refines pose using appearance consistency and centroid alignment. (5) Rendered sharp image from 3D serves as ground truth.
AI.result: A deblurring model trained on GS-RealBlur outperforms models trained on existing synthetic and real-world datasets across various real-world deblurring benchmarks, demonstrating superior generalization performance.
AI.conclusion: GS-RealBlur effectively addresses the realism-flexibility trade-off in deblurring data acquisition, enabling high-quality dataset construction and better real-world deblurring performance.

title: Do Coding Agents Need Executable World Models, Simplification, and Verification to Solve ARC-AGI-3?
summary: Our previous ARC-AGI-3 agent bundled executable world modeling, scheduled simplification, and exact replay verification, leaving unclear which idea accounted for its performance. We address this attribution question with four nested Codex-based agents: a textual baseline; a flexible-interface executable world model without replay verification; the same executable model with scheduled simplification; and a fixed-interface verification treatment that retains simplification and requires exact reproduction of recorded observations. The main study evaluates all four agents with gpt-5.4 and gpt-5.5 at high and xhigh reasoning effort on the public ARC-AGI-3 games. Exploratory follow-ups evaluate the textual and verification variants with gpt-5.6-sol at xhigh and max. The most robust result is that every agent variant improves with a stronger model and with greater reasoning effort. Within each model-effort setting, differences among variants are smaller than anticipated, while the effects of individual components vary across settings. Requiring a persistent executable deliverable is not universally beneficial: the textual variant outperforms the flexible-interface executable variant in both gpt-5.5 settings. Simplification improves performance in three of the four model-effort settings, with the weakest setting as the only exception. The complete verification treatment ranks first in all four settings, although it uses substantially more resources. In the gpt-5.6-sol follow-up, the verification variant fully solves every public game at both reasoning efforts, achieves about 99% RHAE, and uses fewer than half the total actions of the human baseline. Because the model postdates these games and held-out performance remains untested, this result should be interpreted as saturation of the public set only.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically ablates an ARC-AGI-3 agent to determine which components (executable world model, scheduled simplification, exact replay verification) drive performance. Using four nested Codex-based agents tested with GPT-5.4/5.5 and GPT-5.6-sol, they find that stronger models and higher reasoning effort consistently help. The full verification treatment ranks best but is resource-heavy; with GPT-5.6-sol it saturates all public games.
AI.motivation: To resolve attribution ambiguity in a prior ARC-AGI-3 agent by identifying which of its three components (executable world modeling, scheduled simplification, exact replay verification) is responsible for its performance.
AI.method: Four nested Codex-based agents were constructed: (1) textual baseline, (2) flexible-interface executable world model without replay, (3) executable model with scheduled simplification, (4) fixed-interface verification treatment with simplification & exact replay. Evaluated on public ARC-AGI-3 games using GPT-5.4/5.5 at high/xhigh effort, plus GPT-5.6-sol follow-ups.
AI.result: Stronger models and higher reasoning effort consistently improve all variants. Component effects vary across settings. Textual baseline outperformed flexible executable in two GPT-5.5 settings. Simplification helped in 3 of 4 settings. Full verification ranked first in all settings but used more resources. GPT-5.6-sol saturated public games (~99% RHAE, fewer actions than human baseline).
AI.conclusion: No single component universally dominates; performance gains depend on model and effort. The full verification pipeline is most effective but costly. The GPT-5.6-sol result suggests saturation of the public benchmark, not general intelligence, as held-out performance remains untested.

title: From hyperplanes to hyperellipsoids: characterizing the inherent interpretability of linear and single-qubit mixed-state binary classification models
summary: We characterize and compare the inherent interpretability offerings of a standard linear model with a single qubit mixed state model for the task of supervised binary classification. A side by side comparison reveals that a single qubit mixed state model for binary classification is just the ``ellipsoid version" of standard linear model classification. More precisely, rather than learning a hyperplane to classify data, we learn a hyperellipsoid. We discuss the consequences of the geometric inductive biases of both models, as well as how each model contains a different feature importance inductive bias. This short characterization offers an accessible route to quantum machine learning (ML) ideas for readers who have zero background in quantum and are only familiar with linear classification in ML. In support of ML pedagogy, we encourage instructors to utilize this piece to smoothly introduce quantum ML ideas into the undergraduate ML classroom.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that a single qubit mixed state model for binary classification is essentially the "ellipsoid version" of standard linear model classification (hyperplane-based), comparing their interpretability, geometric inductive biases, and feature importance biases, aiming to introduce quantum ML concepts to audiences with no quantum background.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between classical machine learning and quantum machine learning by providing an accessible, interpretable comparison between a standard linear model and a single qubit mixed state model for binary classification.
AI.method: The authors conduct a side-by-side characterization of a linear model (hyperplane-based) versus a single qubit mixed state model (hyperellipsoid-based), analyzing their geometric inductive biases and feature importance inductive biases.
AI.result: The single qubit mixed state model is the "ellipsoid version" of linear classification; instead of learning a hyperplane, it learns a hyperellipsoid, with different geometric and feature importance biases.
AI.conclusion: This comparison offers an accessible route to introduce quantum ML ideas to readers with zero quantum background, and the authors encourage its use in undergraduate ML classrooms for pedagogical purposes.

title: Dual-Security for Indoor OFDM-ISAC Systems via Temporal Artificial Noise
summary: With the rapid development of integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) as a key enabler for future wireless networks, ensuring the security of both communication and sensing functions has become increasingly important. Current secure ISAC studies focus restrictively either on the communication or the sensing security, but not both. To bridge this gap, this paper investigates security for both, i.e., dual-security, in indoor orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) based ISAC systems. Specifically, we consider a scenario in which a sensing user (SU) is authorised for sensing but may eavesdrop on communication data, while a communication user (CU) is authorised for communication but may perform unauthorised sensing. We chose this scenario as the pathological case where an authorised eavesdropper has more information and is more effective than an unauthorised one. To address this case, we propose the use of temporal artificial noise (AN) to prevent malicious CU sensing by enlarging its time-domain sensing error, and simultaneously degrade SU data eavesdropping by reducing its frequency-domain signal-to-noise-plus-interference ratio (SINR) with standard OFDM receiver processing. Meanwhile, our proposed scheme guarantees the sensing performance of the SU and the communication performance of the CU. We present numerical results that demonstrate AN can effectively provide dual protection for sensing and communication in OFDM-ISAC systems while guaranteeing the performance of legitimate users.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a temporal artificial noise (AN) scheme to achieve dual-security—protecting both communication and sensing—in indoor OFDM-ISAC systems, where an authorized sensing user may eavesdrop on communication and an authorized communication user may perform unauthorized sensing.
AI.motivation: Existing secure ISAC studies address either communication security or sensing security separately, not both simultaneously. This paper bridges the gap by investigating dual-security, focusing on the pathological case where authorized users (SU and CU) have more information and are more effective eavesdroppers/attackers than unauthorized ones.
AI.method: The authors propose temporal artificial noise (AN) that simultaneously: (1) enlarges the time-domain sensing error for malicious CU to prevent unauthorized sensing, and (2) degrades the frequency-domain SINR for SU to prevent data eavesdropping under standard OFDM receiver processing, while preserving legitimate performance for both users.
AI.result: Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed temporal AN scheme effectively provides dual protection for both sensing and communication in OFDM-ISAC systems, while guaranteeing the performance of legitimate users.
AI.conclusion: Temporal AN is a viable approach for dual-security in OFDM-ISAC systems, addressing the realistic threat where authorized users leverage their privileges for malicious activities.

title: Energy Efficient Active Stacked Intelligent Metasurfaces
summary: This paper investigates an energy-efficient active stacked intelligent metasurfaces (ASIM)-assisted downlink transmission framework, where a multi-antenna base station (BS) serves multiple users through a multi-layer metasurface architecture. Unlike conventional passive intelligent surfaces, the considered ASIM employs active amplification and multiple transmissive layers to enhance electromagnetic wave manipulation. We aim to maximize the system energy efficiency (EE) by jointly optimizing the BS beamforming and ASIM configurations under user quality-of-service and amplification constraints. The resulting problem is highly coupled and non-convex due to the cascaded near-field channel and multi-layer metasurface structure. To address this challenge, we first transform the original problem through epigraph, Lagrangian dual, and quadratic transformations. An alternative optimization framework is then developed, where the BS beamforming subproblem is solved via successive convex approximation (SCA), while the ASIM configuration is optimized using Bayesian optimization based on a Gaussian-process surrogate model. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed scheme significantly improves the achievable EE compared to conventional passive SIM and heuristic benchmark methods. Furthermore, the impacts of amplification capability, number of metasurface layers, and inter-layer spacing on system performance are investigated, providing useful design insights for future active metasurface-assisted wireless networks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an energy-efficient active stacked intelligent metasurfaces (ASIM) framework for downlink transmission, jointly optimizing BS beamforming and ASIM configurations via SCA and Bayesian optimization to maximize energy efficiency, outperforming passive SIM and heuristic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To improve energy efficiency in multi-user downlink systems by leveraging active stacked intelligent metasurfaces (ASIM) that use active amplification and multiple transmissive layers, overcoming limitations of conventional passive intelligent surfaces.
AI.method: Joint optimization of BS beamforming and ASIM configurations via an alternating optimization framework: BS beamforming subproblem solved by successive convex approximation (SCA); ASIM configuration optimized using Bayesian optimization with a Gaussian-process surrogate model. Problem transformed via epigraph, Lagrangian dual, and quadratic transformations.
AI.result: Proposed ASIM scheme achieves significantly higher energy efficiency than conventional passive SIM and heuristic benchmarks. Impacts of amplification capability, number of metasurface layers, and inter-layer spacing on system performance are quantified.
AI.conclusion: Active stacked intelligent metasurfaces offer substantial EE gains over passive designs, with design insights on amplification, layer count, and spacing provided for future active metasurface-assisted wireless networks.

title: PACE: Persona Adaptation through Conversational Elicitation in Human-Robot Interaction
summary: Equipping humanoid robots with coherent and adaptable personas is crucial for fostering natural, engaging, and trustworthy human-robot interaction (HRI). However, existing approaches often rely on static, hard-coded identities that lack the flexibility to adapt to individual user contexts. In this paper, we present PACE (Persona Adaptation through Conversational Elicitation), a novel framework for the interactive generation and deployment of structured personas on the Ameca humanoid robot. Our system introduces an Interactive Persona Elicitation Pipeline, enabling the robot to dynamically synthesize a tailored, psychologically grounded identity through user Q&A. This elicitation process feeds into a persona prompt compilation phase, generating a structured persona prompt built upon multi-perspective dimensions. We detail the Embodied System Integration required to translate this structured specification into expressive, multimodal humanoid behaviors. Through a comprehensive empirical HRI evaluation, we assess the impact of dynamically generated personas on user trust, perceived anthropomorphism, persona consistency, personal relevance, and interaction quality compared to a generic baseline. These contributions establish a scalable pathway for deploying personalized, interactive, and reliable identities in embodied humanoid assistants. Video demo is available at: https://lipzh5.github.io/PACE/

AI.tldr: PACE is a framework for dynamically generating structured, psychologically grounded personas on the Ameca humanoid robot through interactive user Q&A, enabling more personalized and adaptive human-robot interactions compared to static identity approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing humanoid robots rely on static, hard-coded identities that cannot adapt to individual user contexts, limiting natural and trustworthy HRI.
AI.method: PACE consists of three components: (1) Interactive Persona Elicitation Pipeline (user Q&A), (2) Persona Prompt Compilation (structured multi-perspective prompt), and (3) Embodied System Integration (translating prompts into multimodal behaviors).
AI.result: Empirical HRI evaluation assessed dynamically generated personas vs. generic baseline on trust, anthropomorphism, persona consistency, personal relevance, and interaction quality.
AI.conclusion: PACE provides a scalable pathway for deploying personalized, interactive, and reliable identities in embodied humanoid assistants.

title: Explicit Over Implicit: Enhancing CNNs Via Complex Structure Tensor Representations for Periocular Recognition
comment: arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2404.15608
summary: Our study provides evidence that CNNs struggle to extract orientation features effectively. We show that using the Complex Structure Tensor, which contains compact orientation features with certainties, as input to CNNs consistently improves identification accuracy compared to grayscale inputs alone. Experiments also demonstrated that our inputs, provided by mini-complex convnets, combined with reduced CNN sizes, outperformed full-fledged, prevailing CNN architectures. This suggests that the upfront use of orientation features in CNNs, a strategy seen in mammalian vision, not only mitigates their limitations but also enhances their explainability and relevance to thin-clients. Experiments were conducted on publicly available datasets comprising periocular images (Cross-Eyed and PolyU) for biometric identification and verification in both Close-World and Open-World Scenarios using six CNN architectures. Our experiments on the Cross-Eyed and PolyU datasets yield a 5-26% reduction in EER, providing strong empirical evidence that explicit orientation priors mitigate CNN representational limits in Open-World and Close-World scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that CNNs struggle with orientation features, and using Complex Structure Tensors (compact orientation features with certainties) as input improves identification accuracy. This approach, combined with reduced CNN sizes, outperforms full CNN architectures and reduces Equal Error Rate by 5-26% on periocular image datasets.
AI.motivation: CNNs have inherent limitations in extracting orientation features effectively. The authors aim to address this by introducing explicit orientation priors inspired by mammalian vision, to improve both accuracy and explainability while reducing model complexity.
AI.method: The authors use Complex Structure Tensors containing compact orientation features with certainties as input to CNNs, provided by mini-complex convnets. They test six CNN architectures on Cross-Eyed and PolyU periocular datasets under both Close-World and Open-World scenarios for biometric identification and verification.
AI.result: The proposed approach yields a 5-26% reduction in Equal Error Rate (EER) on both datasets. Using orientation priors with reduced CNN sizes outperforms full-fledged, prevailing CNN architectures.
AI.conclusion: Explicit orientation priors effectively mitigate CNN representational limits, enhancing both accuracy and explainability. This strategy, analogous to mammalian vision, is also beneficial for thin-client applications.

title: Beyond a Joke: Multi-Angle Reasoning for Detecting and Explaining Harmful Humor in Memes
summary: Internet memes intertwine visual cues, textual content, and cultural context, making them particularly challenging to interpret in scenarios where humor, sarcasm, and harmful intent coexist. These complexities highlight the need for explainable meme understanding systems that can provide reliable and structured reasoning to support both accurate classification and human interpretability. However, existing multimodal classifiers either overlook these interdependencies or provide only limited interpretability. In this paper, we introduce MAR-12, a novel framework that leverages Vision Language Models (VLMs) for meme detection and understanding in settings where humorous and hateful elements may coexist. The framework first interprets each meme through twelve structured perspectives derived from humor and hate theories. It then applies a role-aware soft-gated attention mechanism to learn how much each perspective should contribute, followed by a prototype-based classifier for the final prediction. Finally, explanations are synthesized using both perspective-specific reasoning and learned attention weights, ensuring transparent and context-grounded justifications. We evaluate MAR-12 on the PrideMM and Memotion datasets, where it achieves up to 80.3% accuracy for humor detection and 75.9% accuracy for hate detection, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches. Furthermore, both human and GPT-4-based evaluations confirm that MAR-12 produces coherent and persuasive explanations, particularly for memes in which humorous and harmful cues co-occur.

AI.tldr: MAR-12 is a VLM-based framework that interprets memes from 12 structured perspectives, uses attention mechanisms and prototype-based classification to detect humor and hate, and generates explainable predictions, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy (80.3% humor, 75.9% hate) on PrideMM and Memotion datasets.
AI.motivation: Internet memes combine visual, textual, and cultural cues with humor, sarcasm, and harmful intent, making them hard to interpret. Existing multimodal classifiers lack structured reasoning and limited interpretability.
AI.method: MAR-12 uses VLMs to interpret memes across 12 structured perspectives from humor/hate theories, then applies role-aware soft-gated attention and a prototype-based classifier, generating explanations via perspective-specific reasoning and attention weights.
AI.result: Outperforms SOTA with 80.3% humor and 75.9% hate detection accuracy on PrideMM and Memotion datasets; human and GPT-4 evaluations confirm coherent, persuasive explanations.
AI.conclusion: MAR-12 effectively balances accuracy and explainability for meme understanding, especially when humor and hate co-occur, offering transparent and context-grounded justifications.

title: Stochastic Reset Pathfinding: Path-Level Regret for Cascading Bandits over Graph Paths
summary: We introduce Stochastic Reset Pathfinding (SRP), an episodic learning problem on a known directed graph with unknown stationary edge success probabilities. In each episode, the agent commits to a source-to-goal path, and any edge failure during execution resets it to the source. SRP captures settings such as entanglement distribution in quantum repeater networks, payment routing on the Lightning Network, and delivery in unreliable mesh networks. We show that the global-reset structure makes the optimal policy open-loop, placing SRP within the combinatorial cascading bandit (CCB) framework. We propose a Log-Dijkstra meta-algorithm with UCB (PathUCB) and Thompson Sampling (PathTS) instantiations. Our main technical result is a path-level regret bound for PathUCB that decomposes regret over suboptimal paths via a per-path complexity C(pi) combining each edge's prefix and suffix reliability. The bound is complementary to the edge-level CCB bound and more informative on structured graphs with polynomially many source-to-goal paths.
